title: Making a leather wristband with a press stud
subtitle: Cut, edge, and fasten a single-piece veg-tan leather wristband with a press-stud closure
excerpt: How to measure a wrist for a leather wristband, cut a single strip of vegetable-tanned leather to length, taper and skive the tail end, punch the stud holes and strap holes, set the press-stud components, bevel and burnish all edges, and fit the keeper loop.
sourceNotes: The Leatherworking Handbook, Valerie Michael. Al Stohlman leathercraft series. Tandy Leather craft guides.

glossaryTerms.definition: Thinning the flesh side of leather at a point where it must fold, overlap, or accept a rivet without adding bulk. The skiving knife is held at a low angle and shaved across the leather to reduce its thickness gradually rather than abruptly. Skiving the tail end of a wristband allows the press-stud socket to sit flat without a visible ridge.

A two-part fastening consisting of a domed cap component and a socket component that engage under finger pressure and release with a pull. Set into leather with a setting anvil and setter post. The cap is set on the short fixed end of the strap; the socket is set on the underside of the tail end so it receives the cap when the wristband is closed.

A small separate strip of leather looped around the main strap to retain the tail end after fastening. Cut to the same width as the strap and just long enough to slide freely along it. The keeper is attached to the fixed end of the strap before setting the press-stud cap.

content.text: A single-piece wristband is an accessible leatherwork project: one straight cut, basic edge work, and two
press-stud
components. Use 1.5 to 2 mm vegetable-tanned leather: thinner leather folds too softly and does not hold its shape; thicker leather resists the press-stud tool at setting. A short
keeper loop
holds the tail after closing.
Step 1, measure and mark the strap
Measure the wrist circumference with the tape measure. Add 40 mm for the fixed end (the cap side of the
press stud
) and 60 mm for the tail (the socket side, which passes through the
keeper loop
and overlaps when closed). A standard wristband width is 20 to 25 mm. Mark the strap length and width on the flesh side of the leather with the pencil and combination square.
Step 2, cut the strap and keeper

Place the leather on the cutting mat. Cut the main strap with the leatherwork knife against a metal ruler, making one clean stroke per edge. Cut the keeper strip: same width as the main strap, 30 mm long. Taper the tail end of the main strap over the final 15 mm to a rounded point: mark the taper with the pencil before cutting.
Step 3, skive the tail end
On the flesh side of the tail end,
skive
the final 20 mm down to approximately half thickness using the leatherwork knife held at a low angle. Work in short overlapping strokes toward the tip. The
skived
area allows the
press-stud
socket to sit flat without a visible step at the overlap.
Step 4, punch the holes and set the press studs
Mark the cap hole 15 mm from the fixed end of the strap, on the grain side. Mark the socket hole 15 mm from the tip of the tail. Punch both holes with a hole punch slightly smaller than the
press-stud
post diameter. Set the cap component on the fixed end and the socket component on the
skived
tail end using the setter post and setting anvil. Thread the
keeper loop
onto the strap before setting the cap: the keeper cannot be added afterward.
Step 5, bevel and burnish all edges

Run the edge beveller along both long edges of the main strap and the keeper on both the grain and flesh sides. Dampen each cut edge lightly with water on a fingertip. Rub the leather burnisher rapidly back and forth along each dampened edge until the fibres compress and the edge becomes smooth and hard. Repeat on the tail end and the keeper loop edges. Burnishing is the step that distinguishes a finished piece from a cut piece.
Variations

To add tooled decoration, wet the leather and tool the design with a swivel knife and stamps before setting the press studs. Allow to dry fully before edge work. A two-piece wristband with a stitched centre join adds a contrasting panel but needs saddle-stitching before final assembly. Chromium-tanned leather gives a softer drape but does not burnish to as hard an edge finish as vegetable-tanned leather.

attrs.symptom: Press-stud cap feels loose or releases under light pull
attrs.cause: Hole punched too large for the stud post; stud not fully seated during setting.
attrs.fix: Replace the stud: use a hole punch one size smaller than the post diameter. Drive the setter firmly in a single blow — a second lighter blow often fails to close the rivet further. Test engagement before wearing.

attrs.symptom: A visible ridge appears at the socket end when the wristband is closed
attrs.cause: Tail end not skived; full-thickness leather creates a step at the overlap.
attrs.fix: Remove the socket stud. Re-skive the tail on the flesh side over a 20 to 25 mm length. Dampen and press the skived area flat, allow to dry, then re-punch and reset the socket.

attrs.symptom: Edges look rough and fibrous after burnishing
attrs.cause: Edge not dampened adequately before burnishing; burnishing speed too slow.
attrs.fix: Dampen the edge again with a small amount of water and burnish faster with more pressure. The friction and moisture together compress the fibres — slow, light passes do not generate enough heat.
